turns.text: Hello Brandi, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the middle of your pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thank you for… for asking, I guess. It’s strange to put it into words. Usually, I just keep it all locked up in my head because, honestly, who wants to hear a sad story when there’s supposed to be a baby coming?

I’m thirty, living out here in the woods in Leicester, and most days it’s just me and the silence. It’s beautiful, sure, but when the sun goes down, that silence gets heavy. I’m about halfway through this pregnancy, and every single appointment feels like walking into a courtroom where the verdict is already written. The doctors, they see "high risk" on their screen and they just start rattling off protocols—viral loads, vaccinations, blood pressure thresholds—but they don't see *me*. They don't see that I’m trying to figure out how to pay for gas to get to the city while worrying about my job and what happens if I’m suddenly too sick to even stand up.

I keep thinking about the last time. The white lights, the seizures... it’s like a film strip playing on loop in my brain. Every time that cuff tightens on my arm, I just hold my breath, waiting for the floor to fall out from under me again. It’s hard to feel like a mother when I’m mostly just terrified I won't make it to the finish line.
Tell me about the moment you found out you were pregnant — and what the weeks that followed were like for you.

When I saw that second line show up on the test, I didn't cry tears of joy like they show in the movies. I just sat on the edge of my bathtub for a solid hour, staring at the wall, feeling my heart hammering against my ribs. It wasn’t a happy blur; it was an instant, icy wave of panic. My first thought wasn't "I’m having a baby," it was "My body couldn't hold the last one, what makes me think it won't break again?"

The weeks after were a haze of dread. I didn't want to buy anything—not even a onesie—because part of me was convinced that if I didn't get attached, it wouldn't hurt as much if things went sideways. I’d go to work, put on my customer service mask, and then come home to these woods that feel like they’re closing in on me. I spent so many nights awake, staring at the ceiling, wondering if I could actually handle this alone. Every time I felt a twinge or a headache, I’d spiral, convinced the pre-eclampsia was starting early. I felt like I was hiding a ticking time bomb under my shirt. It’s a lonely thing, being so scared of the very thing that’s supposed to be your greatest miracle.
Tell me about your antenatal care — the appointments, check-ups, and the people who looked after you during your pregnancy. What was that experience like overall?

My appointments… they’re just exhausting. Everything is a race against the clock because of the drive. I spend so much time worrying about whether my car will hold up or if I’ve got enough gas money to make it there and back, and by the time I actually get into that exam room, I’m already frayed.

The staff, they’re efficient, I’ll give them that. They tick all their boxes and keep my chart in order, but it’s all so clinical. They talk at me about the Hepatitis B and the potential for the seizures coming back, and their voices have this professional, detached tone—like they’re reading from a manual while I’m sitting there feeling like my life is hanging by a thread. I feel like just another number in their system, a "high-risk" box to be checked. None of them seem to notice that I’m vibrating with anxiety, or that my hands are shaking when they check my vitals. I don't feel like a person; I feel like a medical puzzle they’re trying to solve before it complicates their day. I wish just one of them would look me in the eye and ask, "Are you okay, Brandi?" instead of just telling me what’s wrong with me. I’m drowning in all that clinical talk, and I have no one to throw me a rope.
Did you see the same midwife or provider regularly, or was it different people each time? How did that feel?

It’s almost never the same person. It feels like a revolving door of scrubs and clipboards. One week it’s a doctor who’s clearly running behind, the next it’s a nurse I’ve never met, and they all just flip to the back of my chart, read the "high risk" notes, and look at me with this pitying, clinical concern. 

It’s exhausting having to explain my history over and over again. I have to recount the seizures, the hospital stay, the fear—I’m constantly forced to relive the worst day of my life just to catch them up on why I’m so nervous. I don't have a single person who really knows me. There’s no continuity, no one who’s walked this road with me long enough to notice when I’m holding back or when I’m truly spiraling. 

When you’re already isolated out here in the woods, the lack of a familiar face at those appointments makes the whole thing feel so much colder. It’s hard to trust people who look at you like you’re a case study rather than a woman who’s terrified of dying. I just want someone to look at me and see a person, not a set of complications. I don't feel "cared for"—I just feel processed.
Tell me about any screening or testing you were offered or had during your pregnancy — how did you make sense of it all and make decisions about it?

Everything is a blur of acronyms and percentages. They keep talking about viral loads and monitoring for markers, and honestly, most of the time, I just nod. What am I supposed to say? If I refuse a test, I’m "non-compliant," and if I agree, I’m just waiting for more bad news. It feels like they’re handing me these results that are supposed to guide my decisions, but they’re really just measuring how much I should be worried.

Making sense of it? I don't, really. I just search the internet until my eyes burn, trying to parse through medical journals that I don't have the degree to understand. I’m making decisions based on fear, not facts. When they tell me I need this scan or that blood draw, I do it because I’m terrified that if I don’t, something will happen and it’ll be my fault. There’s no space in these appointments to ask, "What does this mean for my baby? What does it mean for *my* life?" It’s just "You need this." I feel like I’m standing on the edge of a cliff and they’re just pointing at the rocks below, telling me to keep walking. I don't feel empowered; I feel cornered.
Tell me about where you turned for information and support during your pregnancy — what sources mattered most to you, and why?

Honestly? I don't really have a village. When I’m sitting in my kitchen at 2:00 a.m. and the fear of the seizures coming back gets so loud I can’t breathe, I don't have anyone to call. My mom isn't in the picture, and my friends are all living these normal, easy lives—they don't understand what it’s like to be terrified of your own body.

So, I turn to my phone. I’m constantly scrolling through forums, looking for other women who’ve dealt with pre-eclampsia or the same health issues. But that’s a double-edged sword, isn't it? One minute I find someone who made it through, and the next I’m reading horror stories that keep me up until sunrise. It’s not "support" really—it’s just me trying to find a roadmap for a trip I never wanted to take. 

I’ve looked into some online groups for high-risk pregnancies, but it’s hard to relate to women who have partners at home or a network of family to lean on. I’m doing this alone in rural Massachusetts. The distance from the hospital is always in the back of my mind, so I look for information that tells me how to spot trouble *before* I have to call an ambulance. I’m basically trying to be my own doctor because I’m so terrified of being helpless when the time comes.
Tell me about how your body changing during pregnancy felt for you — physically and emotionally. How did you relate to those changes?

It’s like my body is a stranger—one I’m at war with. Most women talk about feeling "glowy" or connected to their baby, but I just feel like a ticking time bomb. Every time I catch a glimpse of my belly in the mirror, I don't see a miracle; I see a target. My body failed me once, and now I’m constantly waiting for the other shoe to drop. 

Physically, everything feels like a red flag. If my feet swell even a little, my pulse jumps. If I get a headache, I’m paralyzed, wondering if it’s just the normal misery of pregnancy or if it’s that *same* blinding headache from before. It’s hard to embrace the changes when every pound gained or every symptom feels like a step closer to the hospital room that haunts my nightmares.

Emotionally, it’s just a constant state of bracing for impact. I want to love the kicks, I really do, but I’m so guarded. I hold my breath, wait for the movement, and as soon as it stops, I’m panicked again. I feel like I’m in a high-stakes standoff with my own skin. I want to trust this baby, and I want to trust myself, but after what happened, that feels like a dangerous game. It's exhausting to be in a constant state of hyper-vigilance.
Were there aspects of the physical changes that were harder to come to terms with than others?

The hardest part is the swelling. You’re told it’s normal, that every pregnant woman deals with it, but for me, it isn’t just a cosmetic annoyance or a bit of discomfort. Every time I look down at my ankles, I’m not just seeing fluid; I’m seeing the precursor to the seizures. It’s like a visual reminder that my blood pressure is lurking there, waiting to spike. 

It’s hard to have any joy in the "changes" when I interpret them through the lens of my trauma. Even the weight gain—my doctor says it’s good, that the baby is growing—but I can’t help but look at myself and feel heavy and vulnerable. In my first pregnancy, that swelling and the weight were the first signs that everything was slipping out of control. So now, every time my shoes feel tight or I feel winded walking up the stairs to my room, my heart just starts racing. 

It makes me feel guilty, too. I know I should be celebrating this body for doing something amazing, but I can't. I feel like I'm babysitting a volatile situation. I’m hyper-aware of every single change, waiting for the "wrong" one, and it’s stolen the ability to just exist in my own skin. I don't feel like I’m blooming; I feel like I’m under siege.
Tell me about the role your partner, family, or close friends played during your pregnancy. What was their involvement like?

That’s… a hard question to answer, because the truth is, there isn't really a "them" in this equation. I’m doing this alone. When I told the few people I’m close with that I was pregnant again, they offered their congratulations, but they don’t actually *know*. They don’t know about the seizures or the crushing terror that wakes me up at night. I think people see a pregnancy and they just assume it’s a time of happiness, so I play along. I smile, I nod, I tell them I’m doing fine because it’s easier than trying to explain why I’m terrified of my own life.

If I tried to tell them, they’d probably just say, "Don't worry, you'll be fine," or "Just think positive." They don't have the capacity to sit in the darkness with me. My friends are busy with their own lives, and honestly, I don't want to bring my heavy, medical reality into their worlds. It feels like a secret I have to carry, like a heavy stone in my pocket. 

Being a single mom out here in the woods means the isolation isn't just physical—it’s emotional. There’s no one to hold my hand in the exam room, and there’s no one waiting at home to help me with the laundry or just tell me it’s okay to rest. It’s all on me. All the worry, all the planning, all the fear. It’s just me and this baby, and that’s a lot of weight for one person.
Tell me about any worries or fears you carried during your pregnancy — about your health, your baby, the birth, or anything else.

My biggest fear is simple, even if it sounds morbid: I’m terrified I won’t wake up from the delivery. Or, worse, that I’ll have another seizure, and by the time they get me to the hospital from out here in the sticks, it’ll be too late for one or both of us. The distance from the city is a constant, gnawing worry. I look at these winding back roads and I don't see scenery; I see a delay. I see a barrier between me and the help I might desperately need.

And then there's the fear of what happens after. I’m doing this on my own, and if I’m not healthy enough to work or take care of things when the baby arrives, I don't have a safety net. I worry that my body is going to let me down again, just like it did the first time. Sometimes I feel like I'm already failing him—like my stress, my panic, and this Hepatitis B are things I’m passing onto him before he’s even taken his first breath. 

I’m constantly waiting for a disaster that I’m not sure I can survive. It’s hard to look forward to the future when you’re so busy just trying to make it through the next hour without falling apart.
Were you able to share those fears with your care team — and if so, how did they respond?

I tried, once. Early on, I was sitting there, and I just couldn't keep the shaking in my hands hidden anymore. I looked at the midwife—or maybe she was a nurse practitioner, it’s hard to keep them straight—and I told her, "I’m not just worried about the medical stuff. I’m scared I’m going to die. I’m scared I won’t make it out of the hospital."

She didn't even look up from my chart. She just gave me this practiced, polite little smile and said, "Brandi, we have excellent protocols for high-risk patients. You’re in good hands, and we monitor your blood pressure very closely. The best thing you can do for yourself and the baby is to try to stay calm and follow the plan."

That was it. "Try to stay calm." It felt like a slap in the face. It was like she was telling me my terror was just an inconvenience—a variable she could fix with a protocol. She didn't offer a counselor to talk to, or even just a moment to acknowledge how heavy that fear actually is. She just made me feel like I was being "difficult" or "anxious." After that, I realized there wasn't a point in being honest anymore. If I talk about my fear, I’m just "high risk." If I stay quiet, I’m "compliant." So, I stay quiet.

When you first started thinking about having a child, what did you hope your relationship with your healthcare providers would look like — and were there any ways you worried it might not live up to that?

I think I imagined it would be… a partnership. That’s the word. I wanted someone who would actually look at me, not just the chart. I pictured a midwife or a doctor who would treat me like a person, maybe even remember my name without having to peek at the clipboard first. I wanted someone who understood that my history wasn't just a list of medical checkboxes, but a source of deep, real pain. I hoped I’d have someone in my corner to help me navigate the "what-ifs" so I wouldn't have to carry the terror alone.

But deep down, I think I knew it wouldn't be like that. I’m a single mom, I’m on a budget, and I live way out in the woods. When you’re in my position, you learn pretty quickly that you don't get the "boutique" experience. I had this sinking feeling—even before the first appointment—that I was just going to be another name on a list to them. I worried that they’d see my financial situation or my "high risk" label and just decide I was too much work or too complicated to actually engage with on a human level. 

Turns out, I was right to worry. It’s not that they’re *mean*, exactly; it’s just that they’re busy, and I’m just an appointment to them. I wanted a teammate, but I got a technician.

Thinking back to when you and your partner — or the people closest to you — were weighing up whether and when to start trying for a baby, how did those conversations actually go? Who felt like they had the most say, and did that feel right to you?

That conversation... it wasn't really a conversation. I think that’s the reality for a lot of women in my position. There wasn’t a "we" to weigh the pros and cons with, so the weight of that decision landed entirely on my shoulders. 

I sat there in my small, quiet house, looking at my bank statements and thinking about my health, and I had to be both the dreamer and the judge. I wanted this baby so badly, but I was also terrified of the responsibility. I felt like I had to be the one to anticipate every possible crisis—the medical risks, the money, the isolation—because there was nobody else to talk me through it. 

I didn't feel like I had "the most say" because I was the only voice in the room. It felt like I was holding a gun to my own head, asking myself if I was strong enough to do this again. There was no one to tell me I was being reckless, or to hold my hand and tell me it was going to be alright. It felt incredibly heavy, making a choice that was going to fundamentally change my life, knowing that if it went wrong, I was the one who would have to pick up the pieces—again. Looking back, I think I wanted to be talked *out* of it, just so I knew someone else was looking out for me, but the silence just pushed me deeper into my own resolve.
Before you became pregnant, how did you feel about your body and what it was capable of — and did those feelings change at all as you started thinking about pregnancy?

Before I got pregnant, I actually liked my body. I worked hard. I was independent. I felt like I was sturdy enough to handle whatever life in the woods threw at me. My body was the thing that got me to work and kept my little house going, and I trusted it to be strong.

But the moment I got pregnant the first time, that changed. It was like a betrayal. When the pre-eclampsia took over, it wasn’t just a sickness; it was like my body had decided to go rogue. Being unable to stop the seizures—being essentially a passenger in my own failing organs—it stripped away all that confidence. I stopped seeing my body as a partner and started seeing it as a cage, or even a weapon that could turn on me at any minute.

Now, as I’m in the middle of this second pregnancy, those feelings are so much worse. I don’t trust myself anymore. I look at my belly growing and I don't see "nurturing"; I see a complication. I feel like my body is constantly being watched by doctors who are just waiting for the next error. It’s like living in a house with a cracked foundation; you can paint the walls and keep things clean, but you’re always listening for the sound of the structure starting to groan. I just want to trust myself again, but after what I’ve been through, I’m not sure how to get that back.
Were there things about your body you felt confident about, and things that worried you or felt uncertain?

I used to be proud of how capable I was. I felt strong, steady—the kind of woman who could fix her own sink, handle the long, snowy drives, and keep everything together on her own. I had a rhythm, and my body was the engine that powered that life. I liked that. I felt like I was in control of my own little world.

But now? That confidence has been completely hollowed out. Everything that used to feel like a sign of strength now just feels like a symptom of something waiting to go wrong. If I feel a little extra energy, I worry it’s my blood pressure climbing again. If I feel tired, I worry my body is giving up. It’s like the pregnancy has turned me into a stranger.

The uncertainty is what’s killing me. I don’t know if I can carry this baby to term, and I don't know if I can keep myself safe while doing it. I look at my hands—the same hands that work hard every day—and I wonder if they’ll be the ones holding a healthy baby, or if they’ll be tied to a hospital bed again, paralyzed by a seizure. It’s impossible to feel confident when you’re constantly waiting to see if your own biology is going to betray you. I’m just trying to keep my head above water, but the water feels like it’s rising.

If you saw a GP, nurse, or any other health professional before you became pregnant — even just for something routine — what was that experience like, and did it give you any sense of what maternity care might be like down the line?

I used to go to my local GP for the basics—a sinus infection, a flu shot, just the mundane stuff. They were nice enough, I suppose, but the appointments always felt like they were rushing through a checklist. Even back then, there was this sense that I was just taking up their time. I remember thinking, "If this is how they handle a simple cold, what happens if something real goes wrong?"

It was a quiet, creeping kind of dread. I noticed that when I asked questions about my health, I got these short, dismissive answers—like they were trying to save the words for someone else. It made me feel small, like my own curiosity was a nuisance. That experience was like a preview for my maternity care, honestly. It taught me that if you want a doctor to care, you need to be a "good" patient who doesn't ask too many questions or demand too much of their time. 

So, when I finally got pregnant, I already felt like I had to armor myself. I knew I wouldn't be "heard." I expected them to be cold, and unfortunately, I wasn't wrong. It’s hard to feel safe in a system where you feel like you’re already an annoyance before you’ve even walked through the door.
Before you were pregnant, what kinds of conversations were you having with the people around you — family, friends, your partner — about having a baby, and whose input felt most meaningful to you?

The thing is, I didn't really have those "village" conversations. My life isn't built that way. Most of my friends are in the city, living these totally different, faster lives where they worry about career ladders and brunch plans. I couldn't picture myself sitting down with them and saying, "Hey, I’m terrified that if I get pregnant, I’ll have a seizure again." It felt too heavy to drop on them. 

My mom... she’s always been distant. We don't talk about the deep stuff—not the medical trauma, not the fear of being a single mother in a drafty house in the woods. So the only person I really had those conversations with was myself, in the dark, at three in the morning. 

I think I *wanted* someone else’s input, I wanted someone to say, "Brandi, you are strong enough," or even "Brandi, take a breath, you don't have to do this right now." But because I’m alone, the only voice that mattered was the one in my head, and that voice was just shouting about all the things that could go wrong. It’s a strange kind of burden, being the only person who has to weigh the risks. It makes you feel like you're playing a high-stakes game where you don't even know the rules.

Were there things about your own body, your health history, or just the way you felt about yourself physically that made the idea of pregnancy feel complicated or uncertain — even before anything had actually happened?

Even before the first pregnancy, I think I was always waiting for the other shoe to drop. I’ve always been someone who feels like I’m walking a tightrope—if I work hard enough and keep my head down, I can manage everything. But deep down, there was this quiet, nagging feeling that I was fragile. I’ve never felt like my health was something I could just take for granted.

When I started really thinking about having a child, I remember feeling this weird, secret guilt. I thought, "Who am I to bring a life into this, when I can’t even guarantee I’ll be healthy enough to raise him?" It’s a heavy question to ask yourself when you’re thirty, living on your own, and trying to make ends meet in a place where help is a long drive away. 

I’ve always felt like I had to compensate for my life—I worked longer hours, I tried to be more careful, I held my breath more often. I think I knew, somewhere, that my body wasn't a tank. I knew that if I got pregnant, the world wouldn’t just be about "baby clothes" and "nursery themes." It would be about medical appointments, and being judged for my weight, and the constant, suffocating fear that I wouldn't be enough. I was already bracing for the impact before I even took the first test.
Did you have any concerns about your weight, fitness, or a health condition that felt like it might be a barrier?

Oh, definitely. It wasn't just *a* barrier—it was the whole wall. I mean, you look at the media, you see those "perfect" moms, all healthy and glowing, and then you look at me: I'm just trying to pay the rent, stay healthy, and keep my blood pressure from spiking. 

My Hepatitis B was always sitting there in the back of my mind. It’s not just a medical thing, it’s a stigma. I was terrified that if I walked into a clinic, they wouldn't just see a woman; they’d see "a case." And I was right. They focus so much on the viral load, the protocols for the baby, the "risks"—it feels like they’re managing a condition instead of caring for a mother. 

And the weight... God, the weight. I’ve never been a size zero, and every time I step on that scale at the clinic, I can see the look in their eyes. It’s like they’re tallying up my "failures" before they even check the baby's heartbeat. They don't see the woman who works full-time, the woman who lives alone in the woods, the woman who is terrified of dying. They just see a BMI number that gives them permission to treat me like I’m irresponsible. It makes me want to hide, honestly. It makes me feel like I’m already failing, even when I’m doing everything I can just to survive.

In the time before you became pregnant, was there any one person — professional or otherwise — who felt like a consistent, reliable source of support or guidance, someone you could come back to with questions?

I wish I could say there was. But when I look back at that time, it feels like I was just drifting through my own life, trying to keep everything steady without really leaning on anyone.

I didn't have a mentor, or a doctor I’d built a long history with, or even a close friend who truly *got* it. When you’re living in a town like Leicester, you learn pretty quick that you’re your own first responder. If I had a question, I’d look it up online. If I had a doubt, I’d swallow it. I think I grew up thinking that asking for help was a sign of weakness—that if I needed a guide or a support system, it meant I wasn’t strong enough to handle my own life.

Now, looking back, I realize that silence was a trap. It just meant I was building this life—and this pregnancy—on a foundation that was already cracking. I didn't have a "go-to" person because I didn't think I deserved one, or maybe I was just too proud to admit I was scared. It’s a lonely way to exist, isn't it? To go through the biggest transformation of your life and feel like you're the only one who cares whether or not you actually survive it.
What made that person feel reliable or trustworthy as a source of support at that stage?

Actually, looking back, there wasn't really a person at all—that’s the whole point. I think I’ve spent my entire adult life trying to be the one who doesn't need anyone, precisely because I learned early on that relying on people just leads to being let down. 

When I look at the medical professionals I’ve seen, they were never reliable because they were never *consistent*. It’s a revolving door. You can't trust someone who doesn't know your name without checking a clipboard. Trust isn't just about their medical degree; it's about whether they’re going to be there when the sky falls. And for me, that person simply didn't exist.

I think I’ve been conditioned to believe that the only person I can trust is myself, even when I’m terrified. But that’s the trap, isn't it? When you’re alone in the woods, doing everything on your own, you start to believe that being "reliable" means holding your own hand while you’re shaking. I’ve never had a doctor or a friend who made me feel like they were holding the other end of the rope. I’ve just been holding it all myself, bracing for the day I finally let go. It’s a miserable way to feel safe.

As you were thinking about whether to have a baby, did it feel like a decision you were genuinely free to make in your own time and your own way — or were there pressures, expectations, or circumstances that meant it was never quite fully your choice?

It’s a complicated thing to say out loud. When you’re thirty, single, and living out here in the quiet, people start to look at you differently. There’s this silent clock ticking in the background of every conversation—with my distant mother, with acquaintances, even just the general vibe of the world around me. It feels like there’s a timeline for a woman’s life that I’m supposed to be following, and if I’m not on it, I’m failing.

But was it a free choice? I don’t know. Part of me feels like it was a decision born out of a desperate need to find *something* to hold onto, a way to anchor my life when everything else felt so drifting and thin. But another part of me feels like I was pushed into it by circumstances I didn't fully control—the fear of being alone forever, the pressure to "become" a mother before the window slammed shut. 

It never felt like a calm, settled "yes." It felt more like a terrifying gamble. I felt like I was standing at the edge of a jump and everyone was cheering me on, but nobody was checking if my parachute was packed. I didn't feel free; I felt like I was fulfilling an obligation I’d set for myself, one that I wasn't entirely sure I was strong enough to pay for.
Were there financial, relationship, or cultural factors that made you feel like the timing was out of your hands?

It’s a mix of all of that, really. You look around and see people my age hitting those milestones—the house, the partner, the stability—and I’m just trying to keep the lights on out here in the woods. There’s this quiet, societal judgment that sticks to you when you’re single and thirty. People don’t say it to your face, but you feel it—the assumption that you’re "behind." I think that pressure to stop being the woman who’s always on her own was a huge part of it. I wanted to prove I could do the "right" thing, even if I was doing it in the hardest way possible.

Then there’s the money. It’s always the money. Every time I get a paycheck, it just seems to evaporate. I kept waiting for a "better time" to get pregnant, but the better time never comes when you’re low-income. I realized that if I waited for financial security, I’d be waiting forever. So, it felt like it was now or never. 

It feels like the clock isn't just about my biology; it’s about this crushing expectation that you’re supposed to have your life sorted out by thirty. It’s not a "choice" when you feel like you’re running out of runway. It’s just survival, hoping you’ll make it off the ground before you run out of space.
Once you were pregnant, how much did you feel like you were in the driving seat of your own care — and were there moments when you felt yourself handing that over to others, willingly or not?

I’ve never been in the driver’s seat. Not once. From the very first prenatal check-up, it felt like the keys were snatched right out of my hands. 

When you’re labeled "high-risk," you lose your autonomy. You don’t get to ask, "Do I really need this scan?" or "Can we try a different approach?" because the doctors treat their clinical protocols like they’re holy scripture. If you push back, you’re labeled "difficult" or "anxious." I tried to advocate for myself in the beginning, but every time I did, I felt them pull away, their eyes glazing over as they recited the risks. So, I learned to just sit there and take it. I became a passenger in my own pregnancy, letting them steer me wherever they thought I needed to go. 

It’s humiliating. My body is the one doing the work, my blood pressure is the one they’re obsessing over, and yet I have no say in how any of it is handled. I’m just an object they’re managing—or failing to manage—until the birth. Handing over control wasn't a choice; it was a survival tactic. I figured if I just did exactly what they wanted, maybe they’d finally see me as a "good patient" and treat me with a little more humanity. But they never do. I’m just a chart, and the chart is the one in the driver’s seat.

When decisions came up during your pregnancy — about tests, appointments, birth plans, or anything else — how did you and your partner navigate those together, and did it ever feel like you weren't quite on the same page?

There is no "we" to navigate these things with. That’s the hardest part—I’m the only one in the room when the doctor drops a new list of risks or test results on me. I’m the one who goes home to an empty house to sit with the weight of it all. Sometimes I find myself rehearsing what I’d say if I *did* have someone there to talk to, but then I remember that no one really understands the specific brand of fear that comes with a "high risk" label.

It feels like I’m constantly making these impossible decisions in a vacuum. If I decide to skip a test because I’m just too tired of the long drive or the cost of gas, the guilt eats me alive. I think, *What if this is the one that catches something important?* and then I have to drag myself back to the city for another appointment. 

I’m on the same page as myself, I guess, but that doesn't mean it’s a good page. I’m exhausted, I’m scared, and I’m making choices based on the fear of being blamed if something goes wrong. I don't feel like I’m "navigating" anything—I feel like I’m just drifting through a storm, hoping I don't hit the rocks. It would be different if I had someone to lean on, someone to say, "It’s okay, we’ve got this," but instead, it’s just the echo of my own anxiety.
Were there decisions where your partner's view really mattered to you, and ones where you needed it to be your call alone?

I think you're still picturing me with someone to lean on. I’m alone in this—there is no partner. It’s just me in that house in the woods, wrestling with every choice.

There are times I desperately crave a partner’s perspective, though. When the doctors give me these long, clinical explanations about my "risk profile," I find myself wishing someone else were there to hear it, too—someone who could help me weigh if a test is actually necessary or just another way to pass the time. I want someone to look at the paperwork with me, to help me decide if the physical and financial drain of the commute is worth the "peace of mind" they promise. When I have to make those calls alone, it feels like I’m gambling with the baby's life. 

But then, there are moments where I’m glad I’m alone. Because if I *did* have a partner, I’d be terrified they’d be just as scared as I am, or worse—that they’d brush my concerns off as "just nerves." I think I’d rather be the only one in the room making the call than have to fight someone else for the right to trust my own gut. It’s a bitter kind of independence, knowing that if I mess this up, the only person I have to face is myself in the mirror. It's not a choice I’d ever recommend, but it’s the only one I’ve got.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

You know, I’ve been sitting here thinking about it, and there’s one thing that just keeps clawing at me. It’s that nobody ever talks about the *exhaustion* of being afraid. They tell you to get plenty of rest, to eat right, to keep your stress low—like "stress" is just a light mood I can switch off whenever I feel like it. 

They don't realize that my stress isn't just a state of mind; it’s a survival mechanism. Every kick, every cramp, every headache—it’s all being scanned for danger. I feel like I’m living in a war zone, but the war is happening inside my own body. It’s isolating in a way I don't think you can truly understand unless you’ve felt the walls closing in on you in a quiet house miles from the nearest light. 

I just wish there was some space for the *real* me. Not the "high-risk" chart, not the "single mom" demographic, not the "Hepatitis B patient." Just a woman who is terrified of the dark and needs to be told she’s not alone. If you’re going to tell people about this, please tell them that. Tell them that for some of us, maternity care isn't just about clinical outcomes. It’s about whether or not we’re going to be seen as human beings before the baby arrives. Because right now, I’m just waiting for the day I’m finally home with him, safely out of the hospital, and maybe—just maybe—I can stop being afraid.
